[]{#Ch18.xhtml}

::: {}
# []{#Ch18.xhtml_p245 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 245. " role="doc-pagebreak"} [18]{.h1_label .display1} [**Literacy**]{.h1_title .display1} {#Ch18.xhtml_Ch18 .h1_head}

*Doris S. Warriner*

DOI: [10.4324/9781003082644-21](https://doi.org/10.4324/9781003082644-21){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {#Ch18.xhtml_sec18_1 .section .introduction role="doc-introduction"}
## **Introduction**

Applied linguistics has always been a problem-solving field. Over the past few decades, literacy studies scholarship has focused attention on when, how, under what circumstances literacy practices emerge, take shape, circulate, and transform across a wide range of contexts. A growing interest in transnationalism, translanguaging, critical literacy, multiliteracies, and multimodality has prompted scholars from the fields of literacy studies and applied linguistics to consider the relationship between literacy practices, []{#Ch18.xhtml_p245_84}language learning, and language use. With attention to the relationship between literacy/literacies (including multilingual literacies, digital literacies, multimodal literacies, and critical literacy) and language learning or use across contexts, this chapter illuminates the promise of drawing on contributions from literacy studies scholarship to pursue the central concerns of applied linguistics.

This chapter explores questions such as 'What does literacy have to do with applied linguistics?' and 'What might a view of literacy as a social, cultural, historical, ideological, and/ or material practice contribute to the study of language learning, language use, discourse, and identity?' The main goals of the chapter are to (1) provide an overview of impactful scholarship from the field of literacy studies and (2) demonstrate that it has relevance to the concerns and interests of applied linguists.
:::

::: {#Ch18.xhtml_sec18_2 .section}
## **Historical perspectives**

Until the early 1980s, the term 'literacy' was commonly used to capture a very specific kind of phenomenon (the skills involved in decoding and producing texts) and the term 'literate' meant the ability to read or write letters and/or symbols in a way that facilitated meaning-making and comprehension. This view of literacy emphasized the cognitive dimensions of meaning-making, phonics (sound-symbol relationships), and individual achievement. Literacy was conceived as a linear and predictable process, and the focus was on the steps of how to acquire or teach the formal literacies of []{#Ch18.xhtml_p245_92}schooling.

Ever since Brian Street conducted fieldwork in Iran in the early 1980s, 'new literacy studies' scholarship have investigated not only the process of decoding sound-symbol-meaning []{#Ch18.xhtml_p246 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 246. " role="doc-pagebreak"}relationships but, also, the social contexts of literacy learning, teaching, and use. [Street (1984)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_102){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_102 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that the various literacy practices that existed in an Iranian village reflected a range of different religious, economic, and educational goals and priorities. Rather than view literacy as a neutral set of cognitive skills acquired by individuals in a linear fashion, he argued that literacy is better viewed as a social practice located in and influenced by a wide range of factors (e.g. cultural, historical, institutional, ideological, political). [Street (1984](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_102){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1995](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_103){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_103 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2003](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_104){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_104 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and other literacy researchers, such as [Heath (1983)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_39){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Collins (1986)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_24){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Scribner and Cole (1981)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_92){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_92 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and Gee (1990), interrogated widely circulating assumptions about what 'counts' as literacy, for whom, and in what contexts.

At the time, New Literacy Studies (NLS) scholarship challenged the universalist claims made by Walter Ong (e.g. that there is a great divide between orality and literacy, and that literacy is superior to orality). According to [Street (1995)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_103){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Ong's (1982)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_81){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_81 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} theory 'turns out on examination to be an account of the particular literate practices of a subculture within his own society, specifically the academic subculture of which he himself is a part' (150). NLS scholarship has demonstrated how important and productive it is to consider the *interactions* between orality and literacy, as well as the many ways in which both are 'embedded in particular social practices' ([Street 1995](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_103){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}: 151; see also [McCarty 2005](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_73){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_73 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Building on the understanding that the *contexts, purposes, audiences, ideologies, and sponsors* of literacy are important, NLS scholars (e.g. [Collins 1986](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_24){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Collins and Blot 2003](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_25){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gee 1996](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_34){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Heath 1983](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; New London [Group 1996](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_79){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_79 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Street 1984](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_102){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1995](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_103){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) examined the contexts in which literacy was defined, taught, learned, and used. In contrast to the *autonomous* or *universalist* approaches to literacy (e.g. [Ong 1982](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_81){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Goody and Watt 1975](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_35){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), NLS scholars adopt an *ideological* perspective on literacy.

A number of important and useful theoretical (and methodological) contributions have emerged from this early work. As one example, the distinction between a *literacy event* ([Heath 1983](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and a *literacy practice* ([Street 1984](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_102){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) has been influential. A literacy event is any event that involves reading or writing, or any 'occasion in which written language is integral to the nature of participants' interactions and their interpretive processes and strategies' ([Heath 1983](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}: 319). Viewing literacy as a social practice with locally specific historical and cultural dimensions, NLS scholars have made important and lasting contributions to theories of literacy. For instance, [Scribner and Cole (1981)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_92){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that the letter-writing practices of the Vai in Liberia can be understood as a set of 'socially organized practices which made use of a symbolic system of systems as well as a technology for producing and disseminating it' ([Baynham and Prinsloo 2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_14){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}: 3).

However, even though theories of literacy have advanced significantly since the 1980s, simplistic generalizations (and myths) about literacy's power and potential remain robust in discourses of learning and schooling. [Collins and Blot (2003)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} point out,

> The central claims of the \[literacy\] thesis are that writing is a technology and transforms human thinking, relations to language, and representations of tradition, a technology that also enables a coordination of social action in unprecedented precision and scale, thus enabling the development of unique social and institutional complexity.
>
> *(17)*

In response, NLS scholars have endeavoured to identify what/whose agendas such assumptions/myths serve. [Gee (1996)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_34){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for instance, identified how literacy myths have throughout history served the political goals of a small but powerful minority, usually to increase their power and control:

> The most striking continuity in the history of literacy is the way in which literacy has been used, in age after age, to solidify the social hierarchy, empower elites, and ensure []{#Ch18.xhtml_p247 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 247. " role="doc-pagebreak"}that people lower on the hierarchy accept the values, norms, and beliefs of the elites, even when it is not in their self-interest or group interest to do so.
>
> *(36)*

Another framework that has had a profound influence on how literacy was defined and understood is critical literacy. According to [Luke (2012)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_69){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_69 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, critical literacy

> has an explicit aim of the critique and transformation of dominant ideologies, cultures and economies, and institutions and political systems. As a practical approach to curriculum, it melds social, political, and cultural debate and discussion with the analysis of how texts and discourses work, where, with what consequences, and in whose interests.
>
> *(5)*

Drawing on Freire's critical pedagogy, critical literacy proponents ask questions such as 'What are we helping students to learn? Why, exactly, do we think it is important that people should read and write? Why is conventional literacy instruction so confining, so oppressive?' By highlighting the 'authoritarian nature' of efforts to provide basic or functional literacy, Freire and others have theorized the restrictive nature of conventional literacy instruction and examined how distinctions based on class, race, and language have long influenced access to quality literacy instruction. Since then, literacy scholars have used the critical literacy perspective to identify, document, and analyze inequities and discrimination in access to schooling and power (e.g. [Luke 2012](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Rogers and Mosley Wetzel 2013](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_88){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_88 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Vasquez et al. 2019](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_108){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_108 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Watahomigie and McCarty 1996](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_117){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_117 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {#Ch18.xhtml_sec18_3 .section}
## **Critical issues and topics**

[Bartlett and Holland (2002)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_10){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} and [Bartlett (2007](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_8){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2008](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_9){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) have examined the different worlds (e.g. life worlds, literacy worlds, linguistic worlds) that might shape individuals' understandings of what literacy is and does in specific contexts and situations ([Bartlett 2008](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Focusing on 'the space of literacy practices' such work highlights the ways that culture is always and everywhere a dynamic, emergent, situated set of practices. [Brandt and Clinton (2002)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_19){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [Hull and Schultz (2002)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_43){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, and [Bartlett and Holland (2002)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_10){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} explored the time and space dimensions of literacies and their influence on the processes, events, and practices involved with reading and writing in a 21st-century world. Many literacy scholars continue to examine the social, material, and ideological consequences of literacy by comparing how things are in theory with how things are on the ground (e.g. [Bartlett 2007](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Brandt 1998](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_17){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2001](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_18){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Farrell 2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_29){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Luke 2004](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_68){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_68 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Martin- Jones et al. 2009; [Pahl and Rowsell 2010](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_83){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_83 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Schultz 2001](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_91){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_91 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Street and Lefstein 2007](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_106){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_106 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Warriner 2007b](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_113){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_113 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Warschauer 2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_116){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_116 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The term *literacies* is now used to represent multiple types of meaning-making practices. According to [Pahl and Rowsell (2010)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_83){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, 'the word literacies signals that literacy is multiple, diverse, and multilingual and spans domains of practice, from home to school to community, and in each domain there are different literacies' (5).

The spatial turn in literacy research ([Leander and Sheehy 2004](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_64){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_64 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) has turned attention to information and communication technologies -- and how they influence what counts as literacy, who gets to participate in which communities of practice, and the material consequences ([Luke 2004](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) of the opportunities enabled by various technological affordances. This scholarship examines the relationship between modes of representation and issues of content, how learners and learning might be influenced by differences in mode, whether 'learning' happens differently when we engage with the world primarily through image rather than through speech or writing, and what is accomplished (e.g. aesthetically, materially, or symbolically) when images supplement or replace writing.

[]{#Ch18.xhtml_p248 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 248. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Such work has also documented and analyzed the kinds of literacies, multilingual literacies, and digital literacies that are associated with new technologies, forces of globalization (and homogenization), and individual or community efforts to maintain affiliations with multiple communities of practice. As [Baynham and Prinsloo (2009)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_14){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, argue,

> literacy goings-on are always and already embedded in particular forms of activity; that one cannot define literacy or its uses in a vacuum; that reading and writing are studied in the context of social (cultural, historical, political and economic) practices of which they are a part and which operate in particular social spaces.
>
> *(2)*

Over the past couple of decades, viewing literacy as a social practice influenced by cultural, historical, and ideological forces has produced work investigating academic literacies (e.g. [Canagarajah 2004](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_22){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Curry and Lillis 2004](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_26){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ivanic 2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_44){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), adolescent literacies ([Lam 2000](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_57){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_59){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lam and Rosario-Ramos 2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_60){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Yi 2008](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_120){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_120 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), bi-/multilingual literacies (e.g. [Hornberger 1989](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_40){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2003](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_41){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Martin-Jones and Jones 2000](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_70){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_70 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Martin-Jones et al. 2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_71){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_71 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Martínez-Roldán and Fránquiz 2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_72){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_72 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Noguerón-Liu 2020](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_4){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Reyes and Azuara 2008](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_86){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_86 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Reyes and Moll 2008](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_87){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_87 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Warriner 2012](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_115){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_115 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), transnational literacies ([Bartlett 2007](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Black 2007](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_15){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_16){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Campano 2007](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_20){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Duran 2016](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_27){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; Jiménez 2009; [Lam 2006](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_58){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_59){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lam and Warriner 2012](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_2){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_2 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Rubenstein-Avila 2007](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_89){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_89 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Warriner 2007a](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_112){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_112 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2007b](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_113){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_114){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_114 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Yi 2007](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_119){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_119 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2008](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_120){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), digital and multimodal literacies ([Alvermann 2010](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_5){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kress 2000](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_54){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2003](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_55){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Jewitt 2008](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_46){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Koutsogiannis and Adampa 2012](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_52){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kovalik and Curwood 2019](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_53){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leander and Sheehy 2004](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_64){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Mills et al. 2010](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_75){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_75 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), multimodal literacies ([Campano 2007](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_20){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Jewitt 2008](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_46){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kress 2003](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_55){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2010](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_56){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), multimodal storytelling (e.g. [Vasudevan 2006](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_109){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_109 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Vasudevan et al. 2010](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_110){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_110 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), artifactual literacies ([Pahl and Rowsell 2010](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_83){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Karam et al. 2021](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_49){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and critical literacy ([Alvermann 2012](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_6){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Janks 2002](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_45){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Johnson and Vasudevan 2012](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_48){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Luke 2012](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Morgan 1997](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_77){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_77 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Rogers and Mosley Wetzel 2013](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_88){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Vasquez et al. 2019](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_108){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Although the study of bilingual literacies, biliteracies, multiliteracies, transnational literacies, and translanguaging is not new, more attention is now paid to these topics in scholarly work exploring literacy and language as social practices ([Bach 2020](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_7){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [García and Kleifgen 2020](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_1){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_1 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [García and Wei 2014](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_33){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Li 2011](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_67){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_67 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Noguerón-Liu 2020](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_80){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_80 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Orellana et al. 2003](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_82){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_82 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). According to [García and Kleifgen (2020)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_1){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, 'a translanguaging approach to doing literacy acknowledges what [Heath (1983)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_39){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} called the protean shapes of literacy events, in which spoken and written literacies are always intertwined and shifting, surrounded by other modes in mutual elaboration' (558).
:::

::: {#Ch18.xhtml_sec18_4 .section}
## **Current contributions and research**

Critical literacy -- or 'use of the technologies of print and other media communication to analyze, critique, and transform the norms, rule systems, and practices governing the social fields of everyday life' ([Luke 2004](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; see also [Freire and Macedo 1987](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_30){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) -- continues to remain a useful and generative lens. By expanding definitions of what counts as literacy, we (teachers and researchers) might begin to recognize and value more types of meaning-making, engagement, and responses to instruction. [Lau's (2012)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_62){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_62 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} participatory action research approach shows that a critical perspective on literacy is important for English learners because it "encourages students to become active readers and writers of cultural texts so that they can create their own meanings in order to shape and transform their social conditions" (325).

With an understanding of critical literacy as 'a way of being, living, learning, and teaching', [Vasquez et al. (2019)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_108){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} argue that the benefits of a critical literacy approach are multiple: []{#Ch18.xhtml_p249 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 249. " role="doc-pagebreak"}'students learn as much about critical analysis from being actively involved in the design and production process as they do from their questioning of texts produced by others' (302--303).

There has recently been a renewed interest in the material dimensions of literacies ([Luke 2004](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Warriner 2007a](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_112){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2007b](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_113){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and also in *artifactual literacies* ([Pahl and Rowsell 2010](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_83){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The notion of artifactual literacies has helped literacy studies scholars theorize the material dimensions of literacy practices, pedagogies, and various contexts of teaching and learning. As [Pahl and Rowsell (2010)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_83){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} explain, it is important to understand the role of artefacts, objects, and multimodality in literacy practices and events; how artefacts connect communities; and the value of artifactual critical literacies. Another influential set of contributions comes out of the careful study of multimodal literacies from a semiotics perspective ([Hull and Nelson 2005](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_42){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; see also [Newfield 2015](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_78){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_78 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Whether the goal is 'rethinking composing' in ways that take into account recent (and upcoming) advances in technology and media ([Vasudevan et al. 2010](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_110){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), theorizing the decolonizing potential of multimodal critical inquiry ([Campano et al. 2020](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_21){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), or revisiting the New London Group's pedagogy of multiliteracies ([Leander and Boldt 2013](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_63){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_63 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), literacy studies scholarship continues to move away from a singular focus on texts, reading and writing to consider a wider range of practices, modalities, audiences, and contexts.

A growing number of studies have advanced researchers' and practitioners' understandings of the situated ways that digital literacy practices intersect with and support the educational experiences and language learning efforts of recently arrived immigrants in the US context. Whether analyzing how teenagers use the Internet to position themselves in particular ways in and through writing ([Lam 2000](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_57){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [McGinnis et al. 2007](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_74){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_74 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), how fanfiction promotes language learning and literacy development by engaging adolescents in meaningful activities ([Black 2007](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_16){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), or the 'biliterate composing practices' of high school students from immigrant families ([Yi 2007](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_119){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2008](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_120){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), this work continues to raise questions about how literacy is defined, by whom, and with what consequences (especially for poor or immigrant youth). Such work opens up spaces for identifying, documenting, and analyzing how those on the move combine multiple semiotic resources in order to learn English, maintain connections with people and practices from their 'homeland', and establish new sets of practices valued in the communities of practice they wish to join. As [Jiménez et al. (2009)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_47){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} argue, 'including transnational and community literacies can help students learn about diversity in their communities and help English-language learners become more fully engaged in their literacy and content learning' (p. 16). Recent work on the digital literacy practices of immigrant youth raises important questions about the intersections between second language learning processes, social identities, and multilingual literacies (e.g. [Purcell-Gates 2013](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_85){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_85 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). It also gives attention to the complicated intersections between learning, language, and literacy for individuals often overlooked in the field of literacy studies. Such work illustrates effectively and powerfully the many common interests and concerns that literacy studies and applied linguistics have and demonstrates the value of identifying and analyzing the digital literacy practices of multilingual, multinational individuals and communities living in a variety of contexts.

More attention has also been paid to 'activist literacies' ([Simon and Campano 2013](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_93){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_93 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); the affective dimension of literacy ([Lemke 2013](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_65){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_65 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wargo 2015](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_111){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_111 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); and anti-racist and/or decolonizing approaches to multiliteracies ([García et al. 2021](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_31){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kinloch 2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_50){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kirkland 2013](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_51){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Schroeter 2019](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_90){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_90 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). As [García et al. (2021)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_31){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} observe, 'we conceptualize pedagogy as more than a series of "strategies", seeing it instead as a way to create in-school spaces that leverage the language and knowledge systems of racialized bilingual students' (16). Whether applying the lens of critical literacies, artifactual literacies, transnational literacies, translanguaging and literacies, or raciolinguistics, the study of literacy requires looking beyond practices of decoding and taking into account multiplicity, complexity, and a nuanced understanding of context (e.g. situational, institutional, cultural, historical, ideological).
:::

::: {#Ch18.xhtml_sec18_5 .section}
## []{#Ch18.xhtml_p250 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 250. " role="doc-pagebreak"}**Main research methods**

Literacy studies scholarship often adopts an anthropological lens and utilizes ethnographic methods to explore (across cultures, contexts, situations, and interactions) what gives meaning to particular literacy events and the practices identified and documented. Researchers sometimes combine microanalysis of language and literacy with a macroanalysis of discourse, ideology, and power. Yet advances in how we theorize literacies, multiliteracies, multimodal and digital literacies, and critical literacy have also influenced the methods and methodologies that researchers use. For instance, adopting a translanguaging stance also typically requires a shift in what is valued or recognized epistemologically ([García and Kleifgen 2020](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_1){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}):

> Taking up a translanguaging framework would mean that the work of literacy scholars would shift its focus so as to bring to the foreground other epistemologies, other imaginaries of the world, other sign systems. Literacy scholars would then decenter the claim that literacy in a dominant language done in the ways of schools is the only key to success.
>
> *(567)*

Examining the 'social semiotic multimodal perspective on learning' has required innovative conceptual and methodological tools. Scholarship investigating social semiotics and/or multimodal literacies demands careful reflection on how to design a qualitative study that allows the tracing of practices in a networked world ([Stornaiuolo and Hall 2014](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_98){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_98 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); how to examine literacies of authorship in social media communities ([Stornaiuolo et al. 2013](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_99){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_99 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); how to analyze mobility in relation to scaling and inequity ([Stornaiuolo and LeBlanc 2016](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_100){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_100 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Smith et al. 2018](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_95){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_95 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); and ways to map the literacy practices used in makerspace ([Hall and Stornaiuolo 2020](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_38){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Stornaiuolo et al. 2018](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_101){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_101 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Smith et al. (2015)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_94){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_94 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} reflect on the potential for 'envisioning' or 'visualizing' to expand what the object of inquiry might be in literacy studies.
:::

::: {#Ch18.xhtml_sec18_6 .section}
## **Recommendations for practice**

In recent decades, the study of literacy as a social practice has taken up 'questions of schooling' ([Baynham 2004](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_13){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Baynham and Prinsloo 2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_14){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Stroud and Prinsloo 2015](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_107){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_107 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) in critical and productive ways. In addition, researchers continue to examine the relationship between literacy as a social practice ([Barton 2001](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_11){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2009](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_12){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and larger cultural, historical, and ideological influences on the ways in which we think, talk, and write about literacy ([Street 2004](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_105){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_105 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and the very consequential 'material consequences of literacy' ([Luke 2004](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) for both individuals and communities. By looking across a range of contexts, practices, and communities, we have advanced our understanding of the many different kinds of factors (cognitive, interactional, ideological, cultural, and institutional) that influence literacy practices in different situations and contexts. Literacy studies research has, over time, contributed to an emerging understanding that students' lives, experiences, and identities cannot be separated (in theory or practice) from their language and literacy learning processes (or outcomes). Teachers and teacher educators have looked for inspiration and guidance from scholarship on critical literacy, multimodal literacy, artifactual literacy, and transnational literacies in order to understand what their students and their families bring to their formal and informal learning experiences.

The long-standing interest in how literacy events and practices are embedded within (and shaped by) particular contexts continues to influence researchers as they work to identify literacy events, literacy practices, and the relationship between events, practices, and the trajectories of individuals and their communities. By going outside schools and classrooms, literacy []{#Ch18.xhtml_p251 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 251. " role="doc-pagebreak"}scholars have gained important insights about the various types of literacies, multiliteracies, multimodal literacies in use, the function of those practices, and the beliefs or views held "by members of communities engaged in them" ([Duran 2016](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_27){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Alvermann (2012)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for instance, demonstrates the value of taking a critical literacy perspective while examining adolescents' digital media practices:

> \[T\]he agency that teachers and learners assume in classrooms where critical literacies prevail has the potential to become nothing short of an antidote to deprofessionalizing mandates issued by distant forces, often with little or no direct knowledge of how schools work.
>
> *(64)*

According to [Alvermann (2012)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_6){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, 'critical literacy instruction that looks at how one is positioned to make a difference, or even which differences make a difference, can open up new possibilities for exploring whose voice counts and why' (65; see also Rogers et al. 2013). Practitioner-focused research by literacy studies scholars has also examined issues of mobility in relation to the use of digital mobile technologies (Ehert and Hollett 2014), the use of digital literacies for liberatory purposes ([Haddix and Sealey-Ruiz 2012](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_36){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and cosmopolitan literacies ([Spires et al. 2019](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_97){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_97 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). As [Johnson and Vasudevan (2012)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} point out, 'teachers must expand current definitions of critical literacy to include a performance lens that recognizes embodied texts and responses' (35). Their analysis of students' existing embodied and multimodal experiences and texts demonstrates the pedagogical value of this perspective:

> This means creating curricular conditions that position students' visible and invisible critical performances at the center of classroom inquiry, that position students as critically literate in a range of modes and genres, effectively producing critical literacies that look and sound different, that surprise students and teachers, and provoke questions about the ways we define, identify, and assess what it means to be critically literate in school.
>
> *(40)*

In addition, there have been advances in the study of bilingualism in relation to digital multi-modal composition ([Smith et al. 2020](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_96){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_96 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and of composing for 'ambient audiences' ([Hall 2015](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_37){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). [Hall (2015)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, for instance, examined 'the social aspects that emerge when writing in a public space like a classroom' (309) in order to understand how an 'ambient audience' influences students' productivity, understanding, and learning. With attention to 'their entire semiotic repertoire' and to 'the complex performances and human activities enacted by multilinguals within social space' ([García and Kleifgen 2020](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_32){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}: 558), teachers and researchers are now encouraged to adopt practices that '\[disrupt\] established monolingual and monoglossic language and literacy understandings to make room for translanguaging' (559). Indeed, [García and Kleifgen (2020)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_1){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} urge practitioners to adopt pedagogical practices that value and use the resources that bilingual students have:

> This pedagogy positions translanguaging as a right of learners to bring themselves with their linguistic repertoires fully into the classroom so as to grow and thrive academically, and it also transforms them from being positioned as inferior learners to being recognized as having valuable language and literacy practices and ways of knowing that extend beyond mandated curricula and standardized exams.
>
> *(559)*

[]{#Ch18.xhtml_p252 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 252. " role="doc-pagebreak"}More recently, [Mohamed (2023)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_76){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_76 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} has examined how Somali refugee families living in Nairobi, Kenya experience and negotiate their religious and secular identities through literacies. Her findings demonstrate the ways that Muslim youth talk about and use specific literacy practices -- and that certain minoritized languages can be valued and prioritized within communities, even by those who do not understand or use the language regularly.
:::

::: {#Ch18.xhtml_sec18_7 .section}
## **Future directions**

Literacy studies scholars continue to actively work to break down binaries, challenge dichotomous thinking, and bridge previously unconnected areas of intellectual inquiry by examining literacy practices/literacies through lenses that broaden their scope of inquiry. In numerous ways, recent and/or current work on literacy as a social practice expands the scope of inquiry to include a much wider range of practices, to show the various ways that literacy practices might be shaped by locally specific dynamics, and to analyze the influence of context. Building on work produced over the past three decades, literacy studies scholarship has recently turned more attention to the influence of rapid technological advancements, the continued movement of goods and people across borders, multilingual and/or multimodal dimensions of literacy practices or events, and growing distinctions between the rich and poor. In addition, greater attention to the embodied, innovative, and emancipatory practices of learners ([Ehret and Hollett 2014](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_28){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Haddix and Sealey-Ruiz 2012](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_36){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Johnson and Vasudevan 2012](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and the semiotic power of multimodality ([Hall 2015](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_37){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Leander and Boldt 2013](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_63){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) has prompted researchers to rethink the theoretical and pedagogical implications of their research findings. Drawing on frameworks informed by critical literacy and critical race theory, [Lemrow (2017)](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_3){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} has proposed a 'theory of creolization' and 'multiraciality' to map race in relation to language, examine translingualism (see also [Canagarajah 2013](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_23){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and to explore the identities of biracial and multiracial students:

> In taking seriously the emergence and portrayal of these knowledge productions at the interstices of race, class, nation, and identity we can see that embracing student voice and artefact offers us not only critical pedagogical reading and writing tools, but legitimate reflections on race and society in the twenty-first century.
>
> *(459)*

Within recent scholarship conducted across a range of different contexts, we can see the continued value of a critical literacy perspective on learning and teaching, as well as increased attention to how engagement in particular literacy practices mediates learning and identity; the growing prevalence of new technologies in such processes; and the relationship between []{#Ch18.xhtml_p252_88}multilingualism, multiliteracies, and multimodalities.
:::

::: {#Ch18.xhtml_sec18_8 .section}
## **Summary**

[Widdowson (2003](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_118){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_118 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}: 14) argued that applied linguistics 'does not impose a way of thinking but points out things which might be worth thinking about'. In contrast, one might say the field of literacy studies does value certain ways of thinking. Moving from a dichotomous distinction between orality and literacy to the notion that, like all social practices, literacies are situated and multiple, literacy studies scholarship has become a field of inquiry that intersects with a number of other intellectual pursuits, including the pursuits of applied linguistics but also those of the social sciences more generally.

[]{#Ch18.xhtml_p253 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 253. " role="doc-pagebreak"}The field of applied linguistics benefits from an understanding of literacy as a social practice that is influenced by cultural, historical, and ideological factors. A systematic examination of literacy practices supports many of the current and emerging research agendas in the field of applied linguistics. Situated at the boundaries between different fields of inquiry, literacy studies scholarship remains both a conceptual undertaking and a systematic examination of empirical data. Sometimes focused on what is going on outside of formal educational contexts, sometimes returning to questions of schooling and pedagogy, and often considering the material dimensions of literacy, literacy studies scholarship examines literacy as a set of social practices that are both local and global ([Prinsloo and Baynham 2008](#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_84){#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_84 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Whether we are examining reading, writing, digital/multimodal literacies, or multilingual literacies, literacy studies scholars are increasingly aware of the need to also examine the role of time and space -- within and across contexts, within and across communities, and within and across practices.
:::

::: {#Ch18.xhtml_sec18_9 .section}
## **Related topics**

identity; language and migration; language socialization; multilingualism; multimodality; social semiotics; translanguaging
:::

::: {.section .bibliography role="doc-bibliography"}
## Further reading {#Ch18.xhtml_r001036}

- [[García, O. and Kleifgen, J](#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_1){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"}. (2020) 'Translanguaging and literacies', `<cite>`{=html}Reading Research Quarterly`</cite>`{=html}, 55(4): 553--571. (This article provides a useful overview of the development of the concept of translanguaging in relation to bilingual and multilingual literacies. It urges practitioners and researchers to consider the actions of bilingual/multilingual students, the importance of metalinguistic awareness, and the implications of research and pedagogy that centre multilingual students.)]{#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_1}
- [[Lam, W. S. E., and Warriner, D](#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_2){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"}. (2012) 'Transnationalism and literacy: Investigating the mobility of people, languages, texts, and practices in context of migration', `<cite>`{=html}Reading Research Quarterly`</cite>`{=html}, 47: 191--215. (This review of research offers a synthesis and analysis of research studies that address issues of language and literacy practices and learning in transnational contexts of migration. Studies reviewed examine practices in families and communities, practices among youth and within educational settings, and practices with transnational media \[broadcast and digital communications\]. This review highlights important role of language and literacy practices in constructing and maintaining social relations across borders.)]{#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_2}
- [[Lemrow, E. M](#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_3){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"}. (2017) 'Créolization and the new cosmopolitanism: Examining twenty-first-century student identities and literacy practices for transcultural understanding', `<cite>`{=html}Journal of Multilingual and Multicultural Development`</cite>`{=html}, 38(5): 453--467. (This article examines rapid demographic shifts in relation to student identities and literacies through the lens of creolization theory -- which, the authors argue, represents a paradigm shift in education.)]{#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_3}
- [[Noguerón-Liu, S](#Ch18.xhtml_bck_ref18_4){.backlink aria-label=" This link returns to its first in-text citation. " role="doc-backlink"}. (2020) 'Expanding the knowledge base in literacy instruction and assessment: Biliteracy and translanguaging perspectives from families, communities, and classrooms', `<cite>`{=html}Reading Research Quarterly`</cite>`{=html}, 55(S1): S307--S318. (This article argues that the literacy practices of emergent bilingual students should be considered and valued in reading curriculum, assessment, and teacher education. It serves as a critical response to 'science of reading' proponents and suggests alternative ways to teach and evaluate processes of decoding and reading.)]{#Ch18.xhtml_ref18_4}
:::

::: section
