::: {}
# []{#Ch23.xhtml_p309 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 309. " role="doc-pagebreak"} [23]{.h1_label .display1} [**[]{#Ch23.xhtml_p309_66}Cognitive linguistics**]{.h1_title .display1} {#Ch23.xhtml_Ch23 .h1_head}

*Bodo Winter and Florent Perek*

DOI: [10.4324/9781003082644-26](https://doi.org/10.4324/9781003082644-26){aria-label="D.O.I. link to this document."}
:::

::: {#Ch23.xhtml_sec23_1 .section}
## **A brief history**

Cognitive linguistics is an interdisciplinary branch of linguistics that studies the intersection of language structure, language use, and the mind. Cognitive linguistics is not necessarily a monolithic, unified theory of language as much as a body of work that is brought together by a particular outlook on language. Historically at least, much work in cognitive linguistics saw itself in opposition to Chomskyan linguistics. The Chomskyan view of language accepts a modular view of the mind ([Fodor 1983](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_36){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_36 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), within which language is seen as an informationally encapsulated cognitive system. In contrast, cognitive linguists assume a less modular view, emphasizing interactions between different levels of linguistic analysis (e.g. between syntax and semantics, cf. §4), as well as interactions between language and other cognitive systems, such as those used for perception and action. Another tenet of Chomskyan linguistics that cognitive linguists largely disagree with is the idea that language is predominantly innate. Instead, cognitive linguists take a usage-based view of language acquisition -- that is, language is fully learned from exposure thanks to domain-general cognitive abilities, without relying on any cognitive blueprints that are specific to language or any kind of innate language ability (J. L. [Bybee and Hopper 2001](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_11){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_11 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lieven 2016](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_78){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_78 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Tomasello 2003](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_123){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_123 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

[Lakoff (1990)](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_69){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_69 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} characterizes cognitive linguistics in terms of two primary commitments: the generalization commitment and the cognitive commitment (p. 40). Of these, we focus here on the cognitive commitment, which is arguably more important for characterizing the field. According to Lakoff's cognitive commitment, an adequate theory of language or the mind must be cognitively and neurally realistic, in line with evidence from other fields, such as cognitive psychology and neuroscience. As an example of this, consider Eleanor Rosch's influential work on prototypes ([Rosch 1975](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_110){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_110 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Rosch and Mervis 1975](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_111){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_111 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), according to which category structure is graded (rather than discrete), organized around radial categories where central members (the prototypes) are better members of the category than less central members. For example, a robin is a more prototypical bird than a penguin, and in Rosch's model, is assumed to be closer to the centre of a category. This work has been hugely influential in cognitive linguistics, which used Rosch's work to reject the notion that categories are defined in a hard-cut manner by distinctive features, as was common in Chomskyan-inspired approaches to semantics. The []{#Ch23.xhtml_p310 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 310. " role="doc-pagebreak"}incorporation of Rosch's work into cognitive linguistic theorizing is an example of how the cognitive commitment was applied in practice.

As pointed out by [Dąbrowska (2016)](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_24){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_24 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, however, cognitive linguists did not always apply themselves fully to the cognitive commitment, focusing mostly on a few key results, such as Rosch's work on prototypes. Moreover, researchers have sometimes contented themselves with saying that their cognitive linguistic theories were in line with existing psychological and neuroscientific evidence without actually demonstrating this empirically. It is also important to stress that there are other approaches that also see themselves as being 'cognitive', such as Chomskyan linguistics; after all, Noam Chomsky was highly influential in the 'cognitive revolution' in psychology in the '50s and '60s ([Chomsky 1959](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_17){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_17 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Thus, identifying cognitive linguistics as uniquely paying attention to results from cognitive science is not a useful way of characterizing the field, and it does not do justice to other theoretical approaches that do the same.

Perhaps the best way to characterize cognitive linguistics, then, is to look at what type of work is commonly conducted under the banner of cognitive linguistics, and what specific theoretical approaches have been developed over time and are considered to be the 'canon' of cognitive linguistics. Seeing language as being non-modular and heavily interacting with other cognitive systems has led to a number of different theoretical approaches that drive research to this day. In this chapter, we highlight conceptual metaphor theory (§2), cognitive semantics and frame semantics (§3), and construction grammar (§4) as three canonical domains of cognitive linguistic inquiry. These by no means exhaustively characterize cognitive linguistics, but they help to get a flavour of the type of work that is commonly understood to characterize the field. In addition, we discuss research on gesture (§5) as one specific growth area for cognitive linguistics to highlight some newer research. Throughout these sections, we point to work where cognitive linguistics intersects with applied linguistics.
:::

::: {#Ch23.xhtml_sec23_2 .section}
## **Conceptual metaphor theory**

Conceptual metaphor theory is one of the core branches of cognitive linguistics ([Gibbs 1994](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_42){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_42 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kövecses 2002](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_67){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_67 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lakoff 1990](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_69){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lakoff and Johnson 1980](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_70){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_70 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1999](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_71){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_71 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In their 1980 book *Metaphors We Live By*, [Lakoff and Johnson (1980)](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_70){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} made the observation that rather than being merely a poetic device confined to specialized literary discourses, metaphor is part and parcel of everyday speech and writing. [Lakoff and Johnson (1980)](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_70){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} aimed to provide an account of the systematic patterns of how metaphors were used in everyday discourse. They noticed that many different kinds of metaphorical expressions appear to reflect the same underlying conceptualization. For example, the English language consistently uses spatial terms to talk about temporal concepts, as in expressions such as *Christmas is coming, Halloween is ahead of us, We are quickly approaching the deadline*, or *We have already moved past that phase of the project* ([Evans 2004](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_29){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_29 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Moore 2014](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_96){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_96 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The fact that not just one expression but several expressions seem to reflect the same connection of space and time led [Lakoff and Johnson (1980)](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_70){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} to propose that there is an underlying Space Is Time metaphor that first and foremost exists in the mind. This conceptual metaphor is assumed to exist independently of language but motivates linguistic expressions such as the one listed. This outlook on metaphor is fundamentally 'cognitive linguistic' because it describes a connection between language (metaphorical linguistic expressions) and thought (underlying conceptual mappings).

[]{#Ch23.xhtml_p311 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 311. " role="doc-pagebreak"}Early work on conceptual metaphor theory was criticized for its circularity ([Murphy 1996](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_98){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_98 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1997](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_99){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_99 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}): a conceptual mapping, such as Space Is Time, was inferred from a set of linguistic expressions, and this conceptual mapping was subsequently used to 'explain' why the linguistic expressions follow a common structure. There are many different ways to answer this circularity concern ([Gibbs 1996](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_43){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_43 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), chiefly by performing experiments in which speakers' behaviour demonstrates the existence of cognitive associations predicted by metaphor theory without directly using metaphorical language in the task (e.g. [Boroditsky and Ramscar 2002](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_8){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_8 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Casasanto 2008](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_13){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_13 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gibbs 2013](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_45){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_45 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gibbs et al. 2004](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_46){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_46 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Meier et al. 2007](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_94){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_94 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Meier and Robinson 2004](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_93){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_93 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Schubert 2004](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_114){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_114 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Winter and Duffy 2020](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_132){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_132 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Winter and Matlock 2013](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_133){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_133 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). With respect to the Space Is Time metaphor, for example, it has been shown that changing the visually displayed length of a line on the screen influences people's judgements about the duration during which the line is displayed ([Casasanto and Boroditsky 2008](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_14){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_14 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), thus supporting the idea that space and time are connected in the mind, not just in language.

Additional evidence that breaks free from the circularity concern uses multimodal data. Conceptual metaphors have been found in co-speech gestures as well ([Cienki and Müller 2008](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_21){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_21 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Müller 2009](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_97){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_97 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Woodin et al. 2020](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_138){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_138 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), such as when moving the hands forward or backward when talking about the future or the past respectively ([Casasanto and Jasmin 2012](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_15){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_15 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Walker and Cooperrider 2016](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_126){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_126 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The fact that not just linguistic expressions but also gestures reflect the conceptual spatialization of time is further evidence in support of an underlying mapping that gets expressed via different communicative channels. Additional non-linguistic evidence for the conceptual nature of metaphor comes from the analyses of pictures, commercials, and films ([Forceville 2002](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_37){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_37 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Ortiz 2011](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_103){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_103 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Sobrino 2017](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_117){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_117 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Winter 2014](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_131){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_131 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). All of this work can be seen as supporting the core tenet of conceptual metaphor theory, which is that linguistic metaphors are at least partially based on underlying conceptual mappings.

Conceptual metaphor theory is also one of the primary reasons why cognitive linguistic approaches generally assume that the mind is 'embodied', which refers to the idea that higher-level processes such as language are deeply connected with and sometimes even driven by lower-level processes such as those used for perception and action ([Gallese and Lakoff 2005](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_39){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_39 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Gibbs 2005](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_44){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_44 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Consider the metaphor often paraphrased as More Is Up, thought to underlie a large range of constructions that use vertical expressions to talk about numerical quantity, such as *high number, low number, rising prices*, and the like. This conceptual metaphor, too, is supported by non-linguistic experimental evidence (for a review, see [Winter et al. 2015](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_135){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_135 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and gestural evidence ([Winter et al. 2013](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_136){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_136 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Cognitive linguists propose that the use of vertical terms to express quantity concepts is motivated by the fact that we frequently encounter a correlation between verticality and quantity in the real world, such as when putting cookies on a pile, where 'more' correlates with 'up' ([Grady 1997](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_53){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_53 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lakoff 1987](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_68){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_68 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Littlemore 2019](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_82){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_82 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Winter and Matlock 2017](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_134){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_134 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In that sense then, conceptual metaphors such as More Is Up are seen as embodied -- that is, directly motivated through language-external perceptual experience.

The use of the term 'embodiment' in cognitive linguistics is, however, not without its critics (see discussion in [Bergen 2019](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_3){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_3 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and it has also been pointed out elsewhere that it is hard to exactly pin down what is meant by the term, especially since it is used very differently by different researchers (e.g. A. D. [Wilson and Golonka 2013](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_129){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_129 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; M. [Wilson 2002](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_130){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_130 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The extent to which the mind and language are embodied is still hotly debated in cognitive science ([Goldinger et al. 2016](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_51){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_51 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hickok 2014](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_58){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_58 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Mahon and Caramazza 2008](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_86){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_86 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and similarly, it is still unresolved to what extent abstract thought actually depends on embodied metaphors and to what extent abstract thought can exist independently of metaphor ([Haser 2005](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_56){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_56 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Winter and Yoshimi 2020](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_137){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_137 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

These open issues notwithstanding, conceptual metaphor theory has profound implications for various branches of applied linguistics. Some work within the cognitive linguistic tradition []{#Ch23.xhtml_p312 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 312. " role="doc-pagebreak"}has looked at how language- and culture-specific metaphors may cause misunderstanding in the classroom ([Littlemore 2001b](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_80){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_80 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); other work has looked at metaphoric competence as a target of second language acquisition itself ([Littlemore 2001a](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_79){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_79 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Littlemore and Low 2006](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_83){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_83 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and as something that should be directly taught. Metaphor has also been hugely influential in discourse analysis ([Cameron and Deignan 2006](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_12){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_12 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Semino 2008](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_115){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_115 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), including critical discourse analysis ([Charteris-Black 2011](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_16){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_16 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hart 2008](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_55){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_55 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Experimental work on what are called 'metaphorical framing effects' furthermore shows that metaphor can change people's political opinions. In a now-classic study, [Thibodeau and Boroditsky (2011)](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_122){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_122 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} presented participants with descriptions of fictive cities in which crime was metaphorically described either as a beast or as a virus. When participants were asked what the city council should do to fix its crime problem, they recommended different policy solutions as a function of which metaphor was used, such as more law enforcement in the beast condition. That metaphor can directly affect decision-making has now been demonstrated for numerous domains, such as climate change ([Flusberg et al. 2017](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_35){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_35 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Metaphorical framing is also investigated with respect to health communication, including the discourse on the COVID-19 pandemic ([Semino 2021](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_116){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_116 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wicke and Bolognesi 2020](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_128){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_128 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {#Ch23.xhtml_sec23_3 .section}
## **Cognitive semantics and frame semantics**

Cognitive linguistics see meaning as conceptualization ([Geeraerts 2006](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_40){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_40 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lakoff 1987](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_68){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), with particular linguistic expressions involving particular construals of the described state of affairs. These construals are often perspectival ([Verhagen 2007](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_125){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_125 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) -- that is, they are seen from a particular vantage point. Studying meaning as conceptualization deviates from the kind of truth-conditional semantics that is more closely affiliated with the Chomskyan tradition. As an example of how cognitive linguists emphasize the types of conceptualizations that linguistic expressions evoke, consider the pair of expressions *The fence runs along the road* and *The fence is next to the road*. Both of these can be true descriptions of the same scene, but only one of them uses motion language. Cognitive linguists suggest that the first sentence, which uses the motion verb *run*, implies a dynamic construal of a static scene, what is called fictive or abstract motion ([Langacker 1999](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_74){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_74 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Talmy 2000](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_120){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_120 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Experimental evidence suggests that people actually perform a mental simulation when reading or listening to fictive motion sentences ([Blomberg and Zlatev 2015](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_5){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_5 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Matlock 2004](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_88){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_88 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Richardson and Matlock 2007](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_109){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_109 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), such as imagining a path that is unfolding, or mentally scanning along an existing path with their inner eye.

Another cognitive linguistic approach to the study of meaning is frame semantics ([Fillmore 1982](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_31){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_31 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Whereas lexical concepts are considered distinct from encyclopaedic world knowledge in many approaches of the Chomskyan tradition, frame semantics breaks this divide, emphasizing that 'a word meaning can be understood only with reference to a structured background of experience, beliefs or practices, constituting a kind of conceptual prerequisite for understanding the meaning' ([Fillmore and Atkins 1992](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_32){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_32 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}: 76--77). This structured background of experience is described in terms of frames. The Commercial Transaction frame for example, includes such things as a buyer, a seller, the goods, and money. Some sentences may instantiate all of these frame elements, such as *The shopkeeper charged the man \$1 for the milk*, where the underlined portions correspond to the frame elements -- buyer, seller, money, and goods -- of the Commercial Transaction frame. Other sentences can omit certain frame elements, thereby backgrounding certain elements of a described scene. For example, the sentence *The man bought the milk for £1* omits the seller, whereas *The shopkeeper sold the milk for £1* omits the buyer. Frame semantics proposes that by virtue of accessing the same Commercial Transaction frame, we recognize that these two sentences can be descriptions of the same scene from different perspectives. Frame semantics has led to the development of FrameNet ([]{#Ch23.xhtml_p313 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 313. " role="doc-pagebreak"}[Ruppenhofer et al. 2016](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_112){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_112 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), a database of such frames as the Commercial Transaction frame that has found applications in computational linguistics ([Gildea and Jurafsky 2002](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_47){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_47 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) and language teaching ([Boas and Dux 2013](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_6){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_6 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {#Ch23.xhtml_sec23_4 .section}
## **Construction grammar**

Construction grammar is the main cognitive linguistic approach to grammatical description and theory ([Fried and Östman 2004](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_38){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_38 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Goldberg 1995](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_48){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_48 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Perek 2015](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_106){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_106 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). The term is best taken as referring to a family of tightly related approaches rather than one unified theory, such as cognitive construction grammar ([Goldberg 2003](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_49){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_49 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), radical construction grammar ([Croft 2009a](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_22){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_22 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), embodied construction grammar (B. [Bergen and Chang 2021](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_4){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_4 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), sign-based construction grammar ([Sag 2012](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_113){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_113 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and fluid construction grammar ([Steels 2012](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_118){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_118 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), to name only a few. Langacker's ([Langacker 1987](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_72){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_72 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [1991](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_73){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_73 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2008](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_75){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_75 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) cognitive grammar should also be subsumed into the construction grammar family, although it was developed somewhat independently and uses quite a specific descriptive apparatus. These many 'flavours' of construction grammar largely agree on their core principles, and mostly diverge in aspects of detail, such as the notation they use, the emphasis that they put on precise formalization, and the specific domain of application that they were designed for (for example, language typology for radical construction grammar, or language evolution research for fluid construction grammar). However, it is important to recognize that one can use construction grammar as a theoretical framework without subscribing to any of these strands in particular.

There are three central tenets shared by all construction grammar approaches: (1) linguistic knowledge is best described in terms of direct pairings of form with meaning (or function^[1](#Ch23.xhtml_fn23_1){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_fn23_1 .noteref role="doc-noteref"}^), a.k.a. *constructions*; (2) constructions can be defined at any level of generality and complexity; (3) constructions are related to each other by inheritance links, and sometimes other kinds of links, into a vast network of constructions. These three tenets and how they relate to each other will be discussed in more detail. There is a fourth tenet that is not explicitly shared by all approaches but can be seen to underlie the three other tenets: (4) grammar is usage-based, in that it emerges through and is likewise constantly shaped by actual situated language use (J. [Bybee 2006](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_9){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_9 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2010](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_10){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_10 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Perek 2015](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_106){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Tomasello 2003](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_123){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In a usage-based approach, grammar is seen as 'the cognitive organization of one's experience with language' (J. [Bybee 2006](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_9){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}: 711), which helps to connect grammatical theory with general-domain cognition.

Earlier Chomskyan approaches to grammar ([Chomsky 1965](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_18){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_18 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) are based on a strict separation between syntax and the lexicon, with the former containing rules to capture the grammatical behaviour of classes of lexical items listed in the latter. Much of the research that laid the groundwork for construction grammar ([Fillmore et al. 1988](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_33){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_33 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kay and Fillmore 1999](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_65){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_65 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) was focused on the study of expressions that are problematic for the Chomskyan view ([Taylor 2012](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_121){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_121 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For example, the expression *let alone* ([Fillmore et al. 1988](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) can be analyzed as a coordinating conjunction when it seems to link two noun phrases, as in *I don't eat fish let alone sushi*, but it can also be used with remarkable flexibility in syntactic contexts where no other conjunction can be found, as in *It would surprise me if John could pass the test, let alone Bill*. In some cases, *let alone* connects strings of words that are not even constituents in the conventional sense, as in *You couldn't get a poor man to wash your car for two dollars, let alone a rich man to wax your truck for one dollar* ([Fillmore et al. 1988](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This means that the rules governing the grammatical behaviour of *let alone* are idiosyncratic: they cannot be defined as general rules of syntax and must be described separately from other areas of grammar.

Another example of such a 'syntactic idiom' is the so-called *way*-construction ([Goldberg 1995](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Jackendoff 1990](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_62){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_62 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), as exemplified by such sentences as *The explorers hacked their way* []{#Ch23.xhtml_p314 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 314. " role="doc-pagebreak"}*through the jungle* and *She talked her way into the club*. There is nothing remarkable in the syntactic structure of these sentences: they consist of a noun phrase subject, a verb, a possessive determiner, the noun *way*, and a prepositional phrase. However, both sentences convey the idea that the subject referent undergoes motion (literal or metaphorical), while none of the words (in particular the verb) entail motion on their own; besides, the motion interpretation does not arise if the determiner preceding *way* is not a possessive co-referent with the subject ([Goldberg 1995](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Perek 2018](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_107){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_107 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}); for instance, compare *He dug a way out of prison* (no motion entailed) to *He dug his way out of prison* (motion is entailed). Therefore, the meaning of motion can be seen as being conveyed by the syntactic pattern described, and thus, a form-meaning pair can be posited.

Early case studies of this kind showed that a constructional analysis is needed for expressions that straddle the border between lexicon and syntax, but they were also quick to point out that the same type of analysis can be extended to structures that are perfectly regular and predictable, such as those described by traditional phrase-structure grammar ([Fillmore et al. 1988](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_33){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). A prime example of this is Goldberg's ([Goldberg 1995](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2003](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_49){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2006](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_50){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_50 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) influential work on argument structure constructions (ASCs), a family of basic clause-level constructions that define how the arguments of verbs are morphosyntactically expressed ([Perek 2015](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_106){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For example, the caused-motion construction ([Goldberg 1995](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}) pairs the syntactic pattern subject-verb-object-locative PP with the notion that the subject argument causes the object argument to move to a certain location, as in *He put the pizza on a plate*. ASCs can be combined more or less flexibly with different verbs, and contribute their own constructional meaning to the sentence, as can be seen for instance when a verb like *sneeze* is used non-conventionally in the caused-motion construction, e.g. *He sneezed the napkin off the table* ([Goldberg 1995](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_48){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}: 9).

Constructions come in many shapes and sizes. They can be simple, like lexical items and bound morphemes (e.g. plural -*s*, agentive -*er*), or complex, like the idiom *pull one's leg* or the caused-motion construction, which consist of several identifiable components. Each of a construction's components, or slots, can be more or less specific, either fixed to a certain form (e.g. *way* in the *way*-construction), restricted to a closed set (e.g. the possessive in *pull one's leg*), or open to a wider range of items (e.g. the verb in the caused-motion construction), with many degrees in between. Constructions can be defined at any level of generality, and more general constructions (e.g. caused-motion) are linked to their more specific instantiations (e.g. caused-motion with the verb *put*) through inheritance relations, forming a taxonomic hierarchy.

Construction grammar has proven to be a popular approach to grammatical description, and over the years has seen use in various domains of applied linguistics, such as the study of second language acquisition ([Ellis et al. 2016](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_28){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_28 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), clinical linguistics and aphasia ([Hatchard 2021](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_57){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_57 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), natural language processing ([Steels 2012](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_118){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and foreign language teaching ([De Knop and Gilquin 2016](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_25){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_25 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In language teaching in particular, there is a growing body of research showing how a constructional approach could benefit language learners ([De Knop and Gilquin 2016](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_25){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Holme 2010a](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_60){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_60 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2010b](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_61){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_61 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Wee 2007](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_127){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_127 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). As argued by [Patten and Perek (2022)](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_105){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_105 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, construction grammar is consistent with many of the most recent developments in language pedagogy, and [Littlemore (2009](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_81){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_81 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}: 169) suggests that construction-based language instruction provides 'a sort of middle ground between the categorical yet inadequate traditional "grammar rules" approach and the more accurate yet potentially overwhelming "lexical" approach'.

In recent years, the field has also seen the advent of 'constructicography', the lexicography of constructions ([Lyngfelt et al. 2018](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_85){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_85 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), which aims to find, describe, and document constructions in various languages and to build constructions: comprehensive inventories of fully described constructions in a given language, typically stored in electronic form. As we are writing this chapter, construction projects in various languages are currently in progress at various []{#Ch23.xhtml_p315 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 315. " role="doc-pagebreak"}stages of development: English ([Fillmore et al. 2012](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_34){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_34 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Perek and Patten 2019](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_108){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_108 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), Swedish ([Lyngfelt et al. 2012](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_84){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_84 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), Brazilian Portuguese ([Torrent et al. 2014](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_124){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_124 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), German ([Boas and Ziem 2018](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_7){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_7 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), Japanese ([Ohara 2013](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_102){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_102 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and Russian ([Janda et al. 2018](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_64){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_64 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In addition to increasing the descriptive coverage of construction grammar studies and providing the approach with wide-scope empirical validation, construction research lines up with the growing interest in construction-based language teaching and in fact supports the wider adoption of construction grammar in the classroom: indeed, if constructions are to be adopted more widely as a teaching approach, teachers and learners need descriptions of what constructions there are to be taught and learned, especially those that are most useful to language users. Accordingly, several of these projects have explicit pedagogical aims ([Patten and Perek 2022](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_105){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Perek and Patten 2019](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_108){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {#Ch23.xhtml_sec23_5 .section}
## **Gesture research**

Cognitive linguistics is a continuously expanding discipline that intersects with various other subfields of linguistics. For example, whereas much past work in cognitive linguistics paid very little attention to the social dimension of language ([Divjak et al. 2016](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_26){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_26 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), more and more work within cognitive linguistics calls for a closer consideration of sociolinguistics ([Croft 2009b](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_23){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_23 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Geeraerts 2016](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_41){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_41 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Here, we cannot do justice to all of the possible extensions of cognitive linguistics. Instead, we highlight one of relatively more 'modern' strand of cognitive linguistics that showcases how the field continues to grow into new directions -- specifically, gesture research. This area of research has been identified as a key growth area for cognitive linguistics ([Cienki 2016](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_19){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_19 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), especially as it interacts with cognitive linguistic theorizing at multiple different levels.

When people speak, they almost always also gesture. Many researchers studying gesture view co-speech gestures as being part of language production rather than a process that is separate from it ([Goldin-Meadow 2005](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_52){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_52 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Kendon 2004](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_66){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_66 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [McNeill 1992](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_92){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_92 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). This view finds a natural home within the cognitive linguistic tradition with its focus on seeing language as an open-ended system that is connected with other cognitive systems, such as gesture. Moreover, gesture research has been influential in work on conceptual metaphor theory because, as mentioned, it is one of the key sources of evidence for conceptual metaphors that does not exclusively depend on language ([Cienki and Müller 2008](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_21){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). Metaphorical gestures have been identified in many different domains besides Space Is Time metaphors ([Casasanto and Jasmin 2012](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_15){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Walker and Cooperrider 2016](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_126){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), including, for example, discourse focused on numerical information ([Winter et al. 2013](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_136){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Woodin et al. 2020](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_138){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), mathematical discourse ([Marghetis and Núñez 2013](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_87){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_87 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Núñez 2004](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_100){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_100 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}, [2008](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_101){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_101 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and transgender discourse ([Lederer 2019](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_76){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_76 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), among many others.

Research on metaphorical gestures has shown that gestures can completely change the interpretation of an utterance. Consider the following question: *Next Wednesday's meeting has been moved forward two days. What day is the meeting on now?* This question is ambiguous and has two possible answers ([McGlone and Harding 1998](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_91){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_91 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), either *Monday* or *Friday*, corresponding to two different perspectives on the Space Is Time metaphor ([Boroditsky and Ramscar 2002](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_8){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). We can think of time either as moving towards us (as in the expression *Christmas is coming*), or we can think of us as moving through time towards temporal events (as in the expression *We are approaching Christmas*). Several experiments have demonstrated that the interpretation of the 'Next Wednesday' question fundamentally changes as a function of gesture, with forward-moving gestures (the speaker moves the hands away from the torso) leading to much more *Friday* responses than backward-moving gestures ([Jamalian and Tversky 2012](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_63){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_63 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Lewis and Stickles 2017](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_77){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_77 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Winter and Duffy 2020](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_132){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).

Gesture also interacts with grammar. Cognitive linguists, for example, analyze grammatical aspect as involving different dynamic or static construals of temporal events ([Langacker 2008](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_75){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), []{#Ch23.xhtml_p316 .pagebreak aria-label=" page 316. " role="doc-pagebreak"}with the English imperfective (e.g. *They were dancing*) involving an unbounded construal of an event in contrast to the perfective (e.g. *They danced*), which involves a bounded construal. This difference in conceptualization has received extensive experimental support (e.g. [Fausey and Matlock 2011](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_30){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_30 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Matlock et al. 2012](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_89){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_89 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}), and crucially, aspectual distinctions are also reflected in co-speech gestures ([Duncan 2002](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_27){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_27 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Hinnell 2018](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_59){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_59 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Parrill et al. 2013](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_104){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_104 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). For example, imperfective constructions may go together with longer and more repeatedly iterated gestures. The interaction between gesture and grammar is also pursued in work on multimodal construction grammar, which extends the idea of form-meaning pairings to include co-speech gestures at the form pole ([Cienki 2017](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_20){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_20 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Mittelberg 2017](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_95){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_95 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Steen and Turner 2013](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_119){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_119 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [Zima and Bergs 2017](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_139){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_139 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}). In fact, there is evidence that certain expressions co-occur with gestures the majority of time. For example, [Woodin et al. (2020)](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_138){.biblioref role="doc-biblioref"} found that in a sample of more than 500 speakers, metaphorical expressions of numerical quantity, such as *tiny number*, co-occur with gestures up to 80% of the time. Finally, given the ubiquity of gesture and its potential to reveal people's conceptualization, gesture research is also highly relevant for applied linguistics, with work exploring its role in second language acquisition and classroom interactions ([Gullberg 2010](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_54){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_54 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}; [McCafferty and Stam 2009](#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_90){#Ch23.xhtml_bck_ref23_90 .biblioref role="doc-biblioref"}).
:::

::: {#Ch23.xhtml_sec23_6 .section}
## **Outlook**

Cognitive linguistics looks back on a big tradition of work that encompasses various aspects of language. The field has generated much insight into how language is structured and how it is used, at different levels of linguistic organization, chiefly syntax and semantics. However, as argued, cognitive linguistics is more of a framework than a unified theory; it is not as monolithic as Chomskyan linguistics, but it has multiple different theoretical approaches, such as construction grammar, conceptual metaphor theory, and frame semantics, being loosely connected via a shared vision of language as a usage-based system that is best characterized by its interactions with other cognitive systems. From this open-ended view of language stem new lines of inquiry for empirical research, such as looking at how gesture reflects conceptual metaphors and meaning distinctions predicted by cognitive theories of grammar. Throughout these further extensions into new territories, cognitive linguistics continues to be relevant for applied linguistics, including such domains as education, second language acquisition, and political discourse.
:::

::: {#Ch23.xhtml_sec23_7 .section}
## **Related topics**

second language acquisition; political discourse; conceptualizing language education
:::

::: {.endnotes .endnotes role="doc-endnotes"}
## **Note**
:::

## Further reading {#Ch23.xhtml_r001051}

- [Dąbrowska, E. and Divjak, D. (2019) `<cite>`{=html}Cognitive Linguistics -- Foundations of Language`</cite>`{=html}, Berlin: Mouton de Gruyter. (This book has chapters on key concepts of cognitive linguistics, including embodiment, entrechment, metaphor, metonymy, blending, and more.)]{#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_1}
- [Dąbrowska, E. and Divjak, D. (2019) `<cite>`{=html}Cognitive Linguistics -- Key Topics`</cite>`{=html}, Berlin: Mouton de Gruyter. (This book provides an introduction to cognitive linguistic analyses of a number of different topics, including polysemy, fictive motion, tense and aspect, and more.)]{#Ch23.xhtml_ref23_2}
:::
